questions.stem: A patient is being discharged on prednisone 40 mg daily for 10 days following an acute COPD exacerbation. Which statement by the patient indicates that ADDITIONAL teaching is needed?

options.text: 'I should not stop this medication suddenly without talking to my doctor.'
'I can stop taking this medication early if I start feeling better.'
'I should monitor my blood sugar more closely while taking this medication.'
'I should take this medication in the morning with food.'

questions.rationale: Stopping corticosteroids abruptly or early is dangerous and incorrect. Even a 10-day course suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and requires a tapering strategy if used longer, and should always be completed as prescribed for a short course. Early discontinuation risks adrenal insufficiency and relapse of the condition being treated. The patient should understand that the full course must be completed, and that stopping suddenly (in longer courses) can cause an adrenal crisis. Statement A is correct — do not stop without medical advice. Statement C is correct — steroids cause hyperglycemia. Statement D is correct — morning administration mimics cortisol's natural diurnal rhythm and reduces GI upset.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Corticosteroid teaching: complete the full course, do not stop early or abruptly. Monitor blood glucose. Take in morning with food. Know signs of infection (immunosuppression). Report mood changes, edema, GI symptoms.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient with type 2 diabetes takes empagliflozin (Jardiance) and is scheduled for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy tomorrow. The anesthesiologist orders 'hold all diabetes medications the morning of surgery.' Which additional concern should the nurse identify and report?

options.text: SGLT2 inhibitors should be held 3-4 days before surgery, not just the morning of, due to risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis
Empagliflozin has no pre-surgical risks and can be given the morning of surgery safely
The patient should receive double the dose of empagliflozin pre-operatively to prevent hyperglycemia
SGLT2 inhibitors should be switched to metformin 24 hours before surgery

questions.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, canagliflozin, dapagliflozin) carry a risk of euglycemic DKA — a form of DKA where blood glucose can be near-normal or only mildly elevated, making it easy to miss. This risk is significantly increased perioperatively due to fasting, surgical stress, and altered carbohydrate metabolism. Current guidelines (ADA, FDA, ERAS protocols) recommend holding SGLT2 inhibitors 3-4 days before elective surgery. Holding only the morning of surgery is insufficient. The nurse should escalate this finding to the anesthesiologist and surgeon for clarification and an updated order to hold the medication earlier. This is an emerging but increasingly important perioperative safety issue.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins) + surgery = euglycemic DKA risk. Hold 3-4 days before elective procedures. Euglycemic DKA is dangerous because normal blood glucose masks the diagnosis. Key teaching: fasting + SGLT2 inhibitor = starvation ketosis + insulin deficiency = DKA without classic hyperglycemia.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse finds a conscious patient with type 1 diabetes with a blood glucose of 54 mg/dL and symptoms of shakiness and diaphoresis. The patient is alert and able to swallow. Which is the CORRECT initial treatment?

options.text: Administer glucagon 1 mg subcutaneously
Administer 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate orally (e.g., 4 oz juice or glucose tablets) and recheck glucose in 15 minutes
Administer 50 mL of 50% dextrose (D50) IV push
Give the patient a full meal immediately to restore blood glucose

questions.rationale: The '15-15 rule' is the standard for treating conscious, alert patients with mild-to-moderate hypoglycemia who can swallow safely: give 15 grams of rapid-acting carbohydrate (4 oz orange juice, 4 oz regular soda, or 3-4 glucose tablets), wait 15 minutes, and recheck blood glucose. If still < 70 mg/dL, repeat the treatment. This is the safest, most physiologically appropriate approach. Glucagon is reserved for unconscious or unable-to-swallow patients. D50 IV push is reserved for unconscious patients or those without IV access who cannot take oral carbohydrates. A full meal is slower to absorb and can cause a prolonged correction — it is the follow-up after glucose normalizes, not the acute treatment.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Conscious hypoglycemic patient who can swallow: 15g fast-acting carbs (15-15 rule) → recheck in 15 min → repeat if still low → then a snack with protein. Reserve glucagon for unconscious/unable-to-swallow. Reserve D50 for severe hypoglycemia without oral access.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes patient about glipizide (a sulfonylurea). Which statement is MOST important to include?

options.text: This medication works best when taken with a large meal
This medication can cause low blood sugar — carry a fast-acting sugar source at all times
This medication does not cause weight gain
Blood glucose monitoring is not necessary since this medication automatically maintains glucose levels

questions.rationale: Sulfonylureas (glipizide, glyburide, glimepiride) work by stimulating pancreatic beta cells to release insulin, independent of blood glucose levels. This means they can cause hypoglycemia even when the patient is not eating — unlike some other diabetes medications. Patients must be educated to always carry fast-acting glucose (glucose tablets, juice) and to recognize hypoglycemia symptoms (shakiness, diaphoresis, confusion). Glipizide is taken 30 minutes before meals (not with a large meal) to time insulin release with carbohydrate absorption. Sulfonylureas often cause modest weight gain. Blood glucose monitoring remains important for safety and titration.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Sulfonylureas = insulin secretagogues = hypoglycemia risk even when not eating. Always carry fast-acting carbs. Glipizide: 30 min before meals. Monitor BG. Watch for weight gain. Avoid skipping meals while on this medication.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse reviews orders for a patient admitted for an elective knee MRI with IV contrast scheduled for tomorrow. The patient takes metformin 1000 mg BID for type 2 diabetes. Which nursing action is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Administer metformin as scheduled since the procedure is elective
Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the ordering provider
Switch the patient to insulin therapy permanently before the procedure
Administer metformin with the morning meal on the day of the procedure and hold only for 24 hours post-contrast

questions.rationale: Metformin is renally cleared. IV iodinated contrast can cause contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN), acutely impairing renal function. If renal function is reduced by CIN, metformin accumulates and raises the risk of lactic acidosis — a rare but potentially fatal complication. Standard protocol: hold metformin on the day of contrast administration and for 48 hours after, then re-assess renal function (creatinine/eGFR) before resuming. Permanent insulin switch is not necessary. Administering on the day of the procedure violates the safety protocol. The provider should be notified to confirm the hold order.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Metformin + IV contrast: hold the day of and 48h after. Risk is lactic acidosis from metformin accumulation if contrast causes AKI. Check creatinine/eGFR before restarting. This applies to any iodinated contrast study.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient on warfarin therapy for atrial fibrillation has an INR of 4.8 (therapeutic range 2.0-3.0). The patient has no active bleeding. What is the MOST appropriate nursing action?

options.text: Administer vitamin K 10 mg IV push immediately
Hold the warfarin dose, notify the provider, and monitor closely for bleeding signs
Administer fresh frozen plasma to rapidly reverse anticoagulation
Continue warfarin at the same dose and recheck INR in 3 days

questions.rationale: An INR of 4.8 represents supratherapeutic anticoagulation with elevated bleeding risk, but no active bleeding is present. Standard management for INR 4-10 without bleeding in a patient on warfarin for non-mechanical valve indication: hold warfarin, monitor for bleeding signs, and the INR will self-correct over 24-72 hours as the drug is metabolized. The provider should be notified to evaluate for dose adjustment. Vitamin K is indicated for INR > 10, or INR 4-10 with bleeding risk factors or active bleeding. High-dose IV vitamin K (10 mg) causes prolonged warfarin resistance and is reserved for emergencies. FFP is for life-threatening bleeding requiring immediate reversal. Continuing the same dose ignores the supratherapeutic level.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Supratherapeutic INR (no bleeding): INR 4-10 → hold warfarin + notify provider + monitor. INR > 10 or any bleeding → vitamin K. Life-threatening bleeding → FFP or 4-factor PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate). Never continue the same dose if INR is out of range.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.
